articles.title: Leave a Trace
articles.summary: Leaving something behind, whether it's feedback on a blog post or a message about a software issue, can make the internet a friendlier place for everyone involved. This is especially true when it comes to discovering and sharing solutions to tough problems, as it highlights signal in a sea of noise and helps others find useful ideas. By leaving something behind, developers can build a learning exhaust that shows they exist and are doing real things with software, which can be valuable to others who may stumble upon their work.

articles.title: So You Want to Define a Well-Known URI
articles.summary: Mark Nottingham discusses the concept of defining a well-known URI and its importance for developers. He notes that well-known locations are useful when clients, such as browsers or crawlers, already know the site and need to discover information about it in an efficient way. However, he argues that specifying a well-known location can solve real problems but may not bring adoption if the protocol doesn't have a problem with it. Additionally, some proposals for well-known locations are misused as URL shorteners or lack consideration for the 1:1 relationship between services.

articles.title: Updating Stacked Pull Requests with git rebase --onto
articles.summary: Updating Stacked Pull Requests with git rebase --onto allows developers to update their pull requests by rebasing them onto a new parent commit, ensuring that changes are properly propagated and avoiding conflicts. This technique is particularly useful when multiple pull requests build off of each other, as seen in the example where a reviewer squashed commit B into commit A, creating a new commit E. By using git rebase --onto with the correct command, developers can inform their child commits about their parent's identity, ensuring accurate tracking and minimizing conflicts.

articles.title: The design of littlefs
articles.summary: The design of littlefs is centered around building a resilient filesystem that can survive power-loss and flash wear without using unbounded memory. Littlefs was originally built to learn about filesystem design in microcontrollers, where 32-bit devices like those used in embedded systems have limited resources. The system differs from others by handling erasing and programming operations differently, with programming being relatively cheap but requiring an expensive destructive operation that can be costly on small flash storage.
